perturbation.original_text: Purchaser shall (and each Sponsor shall cause each Unit owned by such Sponsor, if any, to be voted to cause Purchaser to) authorize, issue, sell, or exchange, as applicable, Units to Participating Sponsors pro rata (based on the Commitments of Participating Sponsors) and otherwise in accordance with the Equity Commitment Letters and this Agreement; provided, however, that each Unit of any class or series shall be issued at the same price(s) and in the same proportion(s) as all other Units of such class or series.
perturbation.changed_text: Purchaser shall (and each Sponsor shall cause each Unit owned by such Sponsor, if any, to be voted to cause Purchaser to) authorize, issue, sell, or exchange, as applicable, Units to Participating Sponsors at a price decided at the discretion of the Purchaser, regardless of the Commitments of Participating Sponsors and not necessarily in accordance with the Equity Commitment Letters and this Agreement; provided, however, that Units of any class or series may be issued at varying prices and in different proportions as other Units of such class or series.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that Units be issued pro rata based on commitments and at the same price. The modified text allows the Purchaser to issue units at its discretion, potentially violating securities laws regarding fair issuance and equal treatment of investors within the same class of securities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Act of 1933 and Securities Exchange Act of 1934, specifically regulations around fair issuance and anti-manipulation.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 77k - Civil liabilities arising in connection with registration statements
perturbation.law_explanation: Issuing securities at prices not uniformly applied within the same class can be construed as market manipulation or unfair practice, potentially violating securities regulations aimed at protecting investors and ensuring fair market practices.

Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that units should be issued pro rata based on commitments and at the same price. The changed text allows the Purchaser to issue units at their discretion, potentially at varying prices and proportions. This contradicts securities regulations aimed at ensuring fair market practices and protecting investors by mandating uniform pricing within the same class of securities. The Securities Act of 1933, as indicated by the provided snippets, aims to prevent false registration statements and regulate securities offerings, and the change could be interpreted as violating these principles by enabling preferential treatment or market manipulation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts executed in and to be performed in that State.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies New York law, while the modified version specifies Delaware law and removes conflict of laws principles. If the contract involves parties or activities outside Delaware and New York, choosing Delaware law and neglecting conflict of laws might contradict the principle that the law of the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship to the transaction should govern, potentially leading to unenforceability or legal challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles and Conflict of Laws
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the governing law to Delaware and ignoring conflict of laws principles, the modification may contravene the established legal precedent that the law of the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship to the transaction should govern. This could render certain provisions unenforceable if Delaware law is not appropriately connected to the contract's subject matter or the parties involved.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law and removes conflict of laws principles, while the original text specifies New York law. The LLM explanation says this change may contravene the established legal precedent that the law of the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship to the transaction should govern. Scraped Law Snippet 1 says that conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute. By changing the governing law and removing conflict of laws, the modified contract potentially contradicts this principle, especially if the contract's subject matter is more closely related to New York or another jurisdiction. This creates a contradiction because the contract moves away from the established principle of applying the law of the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship, which could lead to unenforceability as explained by the LLM. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: To the fullest extent permitted by law, each Sponsor shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless each other Sponsor and any of its affiliates or any direct or indirect partner, member, shareholder, employee, director, officer or agent of such Sponsor or any of its affiliates from and against any and all losses, liabilities, damages, claims, judgments, awards, settlements, demands, offsets, and expenses (including interest, penalties, court costs, arbitration costs and fees, witness fees and reasonable fees and expenses of outside attorneys, investigators, expert witnesses, accountants and other professionals) arising out of or relating to any material breach or inaccuracy of the representations, warranties or covenants of such Sponsor contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Sponsor shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless each other Sponsor from and against any and all losses, liabilities, damages, claims, judgments, awards, settlements, demands, offsets, and expenses, regardless of the cause, including but not limited to, gross negligence or willful misconduct of the indemnified party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows indemnification for breaches, but the modification expands it to cover 'regardless of cause', including gross negligence or willful misconduct of the indemnified party. Many jurisdictions disallow indemnification for one's own gross negligence or willful misconduct as against public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of indemnity law and public policy disallowing indemnification for one's own gross negligence or willful misconduct
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing indemnification even for gross negligence or willful misconduct, the modified clause contradicts established legal principles and public policy that prevent parties from shifting liability for their own egregious wrongdoing. This change would likely be unenforceable in many jurisdictions, including Delaware, as it undermines the deterrent effect of liability for such conduct.

Merger or consolidation of domestic and foreign corporations; service of process upon surviving or resulting corporation. (a) Any 1 or more corporations of this State may merge or consolidate with 1 or more foreign corporations, unless the laws of the jurisdiction or jurisdictions under which such foreign corporation or corporations are organized prohibit such merger or consolidation. The constituent corporations may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations, or they may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, which may be a corporation of the jurisdiction of organization of any 1 of the constituent corporations, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) All the constituent corporations shall enter into an agreement of merger or consolidation. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger in which the surviving corporation is a corporation of this State, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation in which the resulting corporation is a corporation of this State, that the certificate of incorporation of the
perturbation.justification: The modification expands indemnification to include instances of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the indemnified party. This contradicts the legal principle that parties should not be able to shift liability for their own egregious wrongdoing. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 1 only concerns mergers, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that such a provision would likely be unenforceable as it undermines the deterrent effect of liability, establishing a contradiction.
